title: A bill by Senator Dave Cortese that creates civil penalties for social media platforms that circulate child sex abuse material passed the Senate Judiciary Committee on Tuesday nigh

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Social media companies would have two days to remove child sex abuse material after it is reported.",
    "Failure to remove child sex abuse material within two days would make social media platforms liable for civil damages of up to $200,000.",
    "Online child sexual exploitation is on the rise despite advances in technology.",
    "In 2021, the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children’s CyberTipline received over 29 million reports of child sexual exploitation.",
    "The number of reports of child sexual exploitation in 2021 was a 35 percent increase from the previous year.",
    "A recent survey indicated that 85 percent of respondents wanted policymakers to do more to stop child sexual exploitation.",
    "Victims of child sex abuse are more likely to suffer from self-harm, depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress, eating disorders, and substance abuse.",
    "Victims of child sex abuse have reported insomnia and panic attacks, impacting their ability to concentrate in school or maintain employment.",
    "Masha's Law allows child victims to recover civil damages against anyone involved in the production, possession, or distribution of visual depictions of child sex abuse.",
    "The Fight Online Sex Trafficking Act (FOSTA) closed a legal loophole that shielded social media platforms from liability for distribution of child sex abuse materials."
  ]
}
```
